hook.text: Boeing's 737 MAX software flaw killed 346 people in two crashes. It was a product management failure, not just engineering.

buildup.text: Boeing redesigned the 737 with larger engines. The new engines changed the plane's aerodynamics, causing a nose-up tendency.

discovery.text: Instead of redesigning the airframe, Boeing added MCAS — software that pushed the nose down automatically.

twist.text: Pilots weren't told about MCAS. When a faulty sensor triggered it, they couldn't override the software. The planes crashed.

climax.text: Boeing prioritized cost and schedule over safety. The 737 MAX was grounded for 20 months — the longest in aviation history.

punchline.text: Hiding complexity from users can have fatal consequences.

quiz.question: What was the product management failure behind the 737 MAX?
options: Hiding critical software (MCAS) from pilots and prioritizing cost over safety
The engines were too small for the aircraft
Boeing built a completely new airplane design
